title: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith

sections.heading: God: Being, Trinity, and Attributes
sections.html:
The Unknowability of God's Essence and Apophatic Theology

John of Damascus opens the Exposition with a declaration that shapes the entire work: God in his essence is absolutely incomprehensible. No created mind, however exalted — angelic or human — can encompass or define the divine being as it is in itself. 1 Timothy 6:16 describes God as dwelling "in unapproachable light, whom no one has ever seen or can see," and John 1:18 confirms that "no one has ever seen God." This apophatic principle is not agnosticism but a discipline of reverence: the appropriate response to infinite being is not confident definition but worshipful silence at the limits of language.

John distinguishes carefully between the apophatic way (the via negativa, saying what God is not) and the cataphatic way (the via positiva, affirming what can truly be said of God). Both are necessary. The apophatic approach safeguards divine transcendence: God is not finite, not composite, not temporal, not passible in the creaturely sense. The cataphatic approach is made possible by revelation: Scripture declares God to be good, wise, just, loving, powerful — and these affirmations are not mere metaphors but analogical truths grounded in the fact that creatures, as God's works, bear a real though finite resemblance to their Creator. The names of God drawn from Scripture are therefore both genuinely true and necessarily inadequate.

The Trinity: Persons and Nature

John's Trinitarian theology is the mature synthesis of the Cappadocian and Chalcedonian traditions. He insists on the full numerical unity of the divine nature: Father, Son, and Holy Spirit are not three instances of divinity (as three human beings are three instances of humanity) but one undivided being subsisting in three distinct hypostases or persons. The unity is real and prior; the distinction of persons is equally real but entirely within the one divine life.

The personal distinctions are constituted by their relational properties. The Father is unbegotten, the eternal source and principle within the Trinity, though not a temporal prior cause. The Son is eternally begotten of the Father — Psalm 2:7 and John 1:1 together establish the Son's eternal generation as a relation of origin, not of creaturely derivation. The Holy Spirit proceeds from the Father — John 15:26 — an eternal procession distinct from the Son's generation yet equally constituting a real personal distinction. John follows Eastern tradition in ascribing procession to the Father alone, not the Father and Son (contra the Western filioque), though he acknowledges that the Spirit is the "Spirit of the Son" and rests in the Son.

The perichoresis — mutual indwelling or coinherence — of the three persons is John's most characteristic contribution to Trinitarian vocabulary. Father, Son, and Spirit each dwell fully in the others; there is no divine being held in common externally or shared out among them. Rather, each person is wholly God, and each wholly contains and is contained by the others, while remaining personally distinct. John 14:10 — "I am in the Father and the Father is in me" — is the scriptural anchor of perichoresis.

The Names of God in Scripture

John examines the principal divine names in Scripture: Being or He Who Is (the name given at Exodus 3:14, which John reads as the most proper divine name, signifying the underived, uncaused fullness of existence); God (from a root meaning to care for and to see, indicating providential oversight); Lord (the name of sovereign dominion, applied to each person of the Trinity); and names indicating specific attributes — Almighty, Holy, Father, Light, Life, Truth, Love.

John's treatment of divine simplicity is philosophically careful. Because God is not composite, his attributes are not parts of him; rather, each divine attribute is identical with the divine essence while remaining conceptually distinct in our understanding. God does not have goodness as a quality added to his substance; God is his goodness. This ensures that the multiplicity of divine names does not imply a multiplicity of divine beings or qualities hovering above a neutral divine substrate. 1 John 4:8 — "God is love" — is exemplary: love is not something God does or has; it is what God is in his very being, and this is the ground of all created love.

  • Exod. 3:14
  • John 1:1
  • John 1:18
  • John 14:10
  • John 15:26
  • 1 Tim. 6:16
  • 1 John 4:8

sections.heading: Creation, Angels, and Human Nature
sections.html:
Creation Ex Nihilo and the Angelic Orders

John insists emphatically on creation out of nothing. God did not work with pre-existing matter, as Greek cosmogony supposed; there was no coeternal stuff that constrained or shaped divine creation. Hebrews 11:3 — "the universe was created by the word of God, so that what is seen was not made out of things that are visible" — is John's key text. Creation is a free act of divine will and goodness, not a necessary emanation from the divine substance and not a constraint imposed by any external material.

Angels are incorporeal, rational creatures created before the visible world and endowed with immaterial intellect, freedom, and power. John follows the ordering of Pseudo-Dionysius in distinguishing nine orders arranged in three hierarchies: Seraphim, Cherubim, and Thrones in the first hierarchy closest to God; Dominions, Powers, and Authorities in the second; and Principalities, Archangels, and Angels in the third (Colossians 1:16; 1 Thessalonians 4:16). Angels are neither omniscient nor omnipresent; they are circumscribed by place in the sense that when they are acting in one location they are not simultaneously acting in another, though they are not limited to bodies as humans are.

The fall of Lucifer and the rebel angels is treated as the definitive confirmation of angelic freedom. The angels who remained confirmed in goodness after this trial are now established in holiness and cannot fall; their perseverance is a grace, not a loss of freedom. Isaiah 14:12–14 and Revelation 12:7–9 provide the scriptural account of the celestial rebellion.

The Nature of Man as Image of God

John's anthropology centers on Genesis 1:26–27: God made man in his image and likeness. He carefully distinguishes image from likeness: the image refers to the capacities given at creation — rational intellect, free will, the power of self-governance — while likeness refers to the moral and spiritual conformity to God's goodness that is the goal of the human vocation and is to be achieved through the free exercise of virtue and cooperation with grace.

Man is a microcosm — the point of intersection between the material and spiritual orders. The body is not a prison or an embarrassment but a constituent part of the human person, good in itself as God's creation and destined for resurrection and glorification. The soul is the animating principle of the body, not a separate entity imprisoned within matter but the form that makes the body to be a living human body at all. John draws on Aristotelian hylomorphism while Christianizing it: the soul is not simply form in the Aristotelian sense but a created spiritual substance, capable of surviving bodily death and awaiting reunion with the body at the resurrection.

The Soul's Faculties, Free Will, and Original Righteousness

John gives a careful anatomy of the soul's powers, following classical and patristic psychology. The soul has rational and irrational (appetitive) parts; the rational part comprises intellect (the faculty of knowing) and will (the faculty of choosing); the irrational part includes the irascible appetite (courage, indignation) and the concupiscible appetite (desire, love). In the well-ordered soul these faculties cooperate under the governance of reason enlightened by faith.

Free will is a structural feature of the rational soul, not an accident or addition. John insists that without genuine freedom there is no genuine virtue and no genuine sin — only mechanism. God made human beings to be lovers and therefore made them free. Deuteronomy 30:19 — "I have set before you life and death, blessing and curse. Therefore choose life" — assumes and commands the very freedom John is defending.

In the original state, man's rational faculties governed the appetites in harmony, and the soul was governed by God. Sanctifying grace was present not as an external imposition but as the natural atmosphere of a creature living in right relation to its Creator. The fall disrupted all these ordered relations: reason was darkened, the will weakened, the appetites disordered, and the animating presence of grace withdrawn. Romans 5:12 — "sin came into the world through one man, and death through sin" — describes not merely a historical event but a structural catastrophe in human nature that only a new Adam can reverse.

  • Gen. 1:26–27
  • Heb. 11:3
  • Col. 1:16
  • Deut. 30:19
  • Rom. 5:12
  • Isa. 14:12–14

sections.heading: The Incarnation and Redemption
sections.html:
The Hypostatic Union and Communicatio Idiomatum

John's Christology is the most technically precise section of the Exposition, written in deliberate dependence on the Definition of Chalcedon (451) while carrying that definition's logic further. The Son of God assumed a full human nature — body, rational soul, and all the natural properties of humanity — while remaining fully divine. The two natures are united in the one hypostasis (person) of the eternal Son, without confusion, change, division, or separation. John 1:14 — "The Word became flesh" — is the event this doctrine describes: not a transformation of divinity into humanity or a mixture of the two, but the eternal Person personally assuming and forever owning a complete human nature.

The communicatio idiomatum (exchange of properties) follows from the hypostatic union. Because divine and human natures are united in one person, what is proper to either nature can be predicated of that one person. It is therefore proper to say that "God suffered" or "the Son of Mary is eternal" — not meaning that the divine nature was passible or that Mary preceded eternity, but that the one person who is both divine and human can rightly be described in terms of either nature. John appeals to Acts 20:28 — "the church of God, which he obtained with his own blood" — as a biblical instance of this exchange: God does not bleed in his divine nature, but the Son of God truly bled in his assumed human nature, and both descriptions are true of the same person.

Two Wills, the Theotokos, and the Atonement

Against the Monothelite heresy, John affirms that Christ has two wills, corresponding to his two natures. The divine will is the eternal will of the Son, identical with the Father's will. The human will is the created volitional faculty of Christ's human nature, complete and genuine. These two wills are never in conflict in Christ because his human will, while fully free in structure, is perpetually conformed to and united with the divine will by the perfection of his holiness. John 6:38 — "I have come down from heaven, not to do my own will but the will of him who sent me" — expresses the human will's constant orientation toward the Father, not its absence.

The title Theotokos (God-bearer, or Mother of God) for Mary is, for John, a Christological title before it is a Marian one. Because Mary bore not a human person who was later united to divinity but the eternal Son of God personally incarnate, she is rightly called the bearer of God. To deny this title is to deny the hypostatic union. Luke 1:43 — Elizabeth's exclamation, "Why is this granted to me that the mother of my Lord should come to me?" — grounds the title in apostolic usage. Mary is honored above all creatures not as a co-redemptrix but as the instrument through whom the Redeemer entered human history.

The Atonement is presented in terms of recapitulation, ransom, and substitution — held together rather than played off against one another. Through the Incarnation, the Son of God took up the full human nature that had been broken by sin; through his death, he paid the penalty sin demanded and broke the power of death; through the Resurrection, he transformed human nature itself, filling it with divine life and immortality. Hebrews 2:14–15 — Christ partook of flesh and blood "that through death he might destroy the one who has the power of death" — encapsulates this logic.

Sacraments, Resurrection, and the Veneration of Icons

John treats baptism and the Eucharist as the primary sacramental means by which the benefits of the Incarnation and Atonement are communicated to believers. Baptism is a death and resurrection with Christ (Romans 6:3–4), a washing of regeneration by the Holy Spirit (Titus 3:5), and an incorporation into the body of Christ. The Eucharist is a genuine participation in the body and blood of Christ: John 6:53–55 — "Unless you eat the flesh of the Son of Man and drink his blood, you have no life in you" — is read as the warrant for Eucharistic realism. The bread and wine become the body and blood through the epiclesis of the Holy Spirit, not by a philosophical transformation of substance but by divine power that surpasses human comprehension.

The final resurrection receives sustained treatment. Every human being will be raised in the body — the very body that lived and died — and will stand before Christ's judgment seat. John 5:28–29 — "an hour is coming when all who are in the tombs will hear his voice and come out" — leaves no room for purely spiritual resurrection. The body's participation in this life's choices means the body shares in the final judgment. The righteous receive glorified bodies fitted for eternal communion with God; the unrighteous receive resurrection to condemnation.

John's defense of the veneration of icons, written in the context of the Iconoclast Controversy, grounds itself squarely in the Incarnation. The Iconoclasts appealed to the Second Commandment's prohibition of images (Exodus 20:4). John's reply is that the prohibition targeted the making of images to represent the invisible and incomprehensible God — a God who had not yet been seen. With the Incarnation, the eternal Son became visible, circumscribed, and depictable in his human nature. To depict Christ in his humanity is not to represent the incomprehensible divine essence but to affirm the reality of the Incarnation itself. To refuse such depiction, John argues, is ironically a concession to Docetism — as if the humanity of Christ were not fully real and therefore not fully depictable. Veneration of the icon passes through the image to its prototype; the honor rendered is not to wood and pigment but to the person depicted.

  • John 1:14
  • John 6:38
  • John 6:53–55
  • John 5:28–29
  • Luke 1:43
  • Acts 20:28
  • Rom. 6:3–4
  • Heb. 2:14–15
  • Titus 3:5
  • Exod. 20:4
